item.title: 【How OpenRouter and Grafana Cloud bring observability to LLM-powered applications】OpenRouter 和 Grafana Cloud 如何为 LLM 支持的应用程序带来可观察性
【Instrument zero‑code observability for LLMs and agents on Kubernetes】为 Kubernetes 上的 LLM 和代理提供零代码可观察性
item.description:
【

Note: The world is changing all around us thanks to AI. Today, anyone and everyone can be a developer, using LLMs to create LLM-powered applications, which users can then interact with by using even more LLMs. 

Observability practitioners need to adapt and they need the right tools for the job. In this series, we'll show you how to use Grafana Cloud to monitor AI applications, including workloads in production, AI agents, MCP servers, and zero-code LLMs (this post).

Building AI services with large language models and agentic frameworks often means running complex microservices on Kubernetes. Observability is vital, but instrumenting every pod in a distributed system can quickly become a maintenance nightmare. 

OpenLIT Operator solves this problem by automatically injecting OpenTelemetry instrumentation into your AI workloads—no code changes or image rebuilds required. When combined with AI Observability in Grafana Cloud, you can monitor costs, latency, token usage, and agent workflows across your entire cluster in minutes.

In this final post in our AI Observability series, we'll show you how to easily get started by combining OpenLIT Operator and Grafana Cloud to enable zero-code observability for your AI workloads.

Why zero‑code instrumentation matters

Traditional observability relies on developers adding instrumentation libraries to their application code. But in the fast‑moving world of generative AI, your stack might include multiple model providers, agent frameworks, vector databases, and custom tools. Keeping instrumentation up to date across all these components is a burden. 

The OpenLIT Operator brings zero‑code AI observability to Kubernetes. It automatically injects and configures OpenTelemetry instrumentation into your pods, producing distributed traces and metrics without any code changes. Because it is built on OpenTelemetry standards, it integrates with existing observability infrastructure and allows you to switch between providers (OpenLIT, OpenInference, OpenLLMetry, custom) without redeploying your applications.

This zero‑code approach is designed specifically for AI workloads. It provides seamless observability for LLMs, vector databases, and agentic frameworks running in Kubernetes. You can track token usage, monitor agent workflows, measure response times, and debug AI framework interactions—all without touching your code.

Benefits of zero‑code observability in Grafana Cloud

There are also multiple reasons why you should use zero-code observability in Grafana Cloud.

  • Rapid onboarding: Deploy the OpenLIT Operator once and instrument all your AI workloads without modifying a single line of code.
  • Comprehensive coverage: The operator supports major LLM providers, vector databases, and agent frameworks, and can be extended to other providers through its plugin architecture.
  • Vendor neutrality: Built on OpenTelemetry, the operator allows you to send telemetry to Grafana Cloud, a self‑hosted OpenTelemetry collector, or any OTLP‑compatible backend.
  • Cost and performance insights: Distributed traces capture token usage, cost, latency, and agent step sequences, enabling you to optimise model selection and resource allocation.

How to set up zero-code observability for AI applications in Grafana Cloud

Now that we've covered why you should be using Grafana Cloud for zero-code observability, let's look at how you can make that happen, starting with a high-level explanation of the workflow, followed by step-by-step instructions for getting started quickly.

And if you get stuck anywhere along the way or need help with your own setup, click on the pulsar icon in the top-right corner of the Grafana Cloud UI to open a chat with Grafana Assistant, our purpose-built LLM that can help troubleshoot incidents, manage dashboards, and answer product questions.

Architecture overview

AI applications like LLMs and agents run inside pods in your Kubernetes cluster. The OpenLIT Operator continuously monitors these pods and checks them against your instrumentation policies. When it finds a matching pod, it automatically injects an init container that sets up OpenTelemetry instrumentation, enabling observability without requiring manual changes to your application code.

Telemetry is sent to an OpenLIT collector or directly to Grafana Cloud’s OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) gateway. The AI Observability dashboards in Grafana Cloud then visualize latency, cost, and quality metrics.

The workflow consists of four key pieces:

  1. AI workloads: Pods running LLMs, vector DBs, or agent frameworks such as LangChain, CrewAI, or OpenAI Agents. The operator supports a wide range of LLM providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, AWS Bedrock, Mistral) and frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, Haystack, DSPy, and more).
  2. OpenLIT Operator: A Kubernetes operator that injects OpenTelemetry instrumentation into selected pods based on label selectors. The operator is OpenTelemetry‑native and allows you to switch providers without changing your application code.
  3. OpenLIT collector: Collects traces and metrics from instrumented pods. You can run it in‑cluster via Helm or send telemetry directly to Grafana Cloud’s OTLP endpoint.
  4. Grafana Cloud: Stores traces in Tempo and metrics in Prometheus through our fully managed platform. Our AI observability solution provides pre‑built dashboards for GenAI, vector DBs, agents, and Model Context Protocol (MCP), allowing you to explore latency percentiles, token and cost metrics, agent step sequences, and evaluation results.

Step 1: Add the AI Observability integration

Before instrumenting your cluster, add the AI Observability integration to your Grafana Cloud stack. This can be done by clicking on Connections in the left-side menu and following the steps outlined in our documentation.

This provisions dashboards and sets up a managed OTLP gateway for receiving your traces and metrics. Once telemetry arrives, the dashboards populate automatically with request rates, latency distributions, and cost summaries.

Step 2: Prepare your Kubernetes environment

To follow this guide, you’ll need a Kubernetes cluster with cluster‑admin privileges, Helm, and kubectl configured. If you don’t have a cluster, you can create one locally using k3d or minikube. For a quick test drive, create a cluster with:

k3d cluster create openlit-demo

Step 3: Deploy OpenLIT Operator

First add the OpenLIT Helm repository and update your charts:

helm repo add openlit https://openlit.github.io/helm/
helm repo update

Install the OpenLIT Operator to enable zero‑code instrumentation:

helm install openlit-operator openlit/openlit-operator

Verify that the operator pod is running:

kubectl get pods -n openlit -l app.kubernetes.io/name=openlit-operator

You should see the operator in a Running state.

Step 4: Create an AutoInstrumentation resource

The AutoInstrumentation custom resource defines which pods to instrument and how to configure the injected instrumentation. It specifies label selectors to target your AI applications, the instrumentation provider (OpenLIT by default), and the OTLP endpoint to send telemetry.

Here is a minimal example that instruments pods labeled instrumentation=openlit and sends data to Grafana Cloud:

apiVersion: openlit.io/v1alpha1
kind: AutoInstrumentation
metadata:
name: grafana-observability
namespace: default
spec:
selector:
matchLabels:
instrumentation: openlit
python:
instrumentation:
enabled: true
otlp:
endpoint: "https://otlp-gateway-.grafana.net/otlp" # Grafana OTLP gateway
headers:
Authorization: "Basic " # Replace with base64‑encoded instanceID:token
resource:
attributes:
deployment.environment: "production"
service.namespace: "ai-services"

Apply the manifest:

kubectl apply -f autoinstrumentation.yaml

Already have AI applications running? Restart the pods that match your selector to pick up the injected instrumentation:

kubectl rollout restart deployment your-deployment-name

When the pods restart, the OpenLIT Operator automatically injects an init container that configures Python instrumentation. The pods begin emitting distributed traces with LLM costs, token usage, and agent performance metrics.

Step 5: Deploy your AI application (no code changes)

You can now deploy or continue running your AI workloads normally. Whether you’re using OpenAI Agents SDK, CrewAI, LangChain, or a custom Python service, you don’t need to modify your code. The operator recognizes supported frameworks and model providers, and it instruments them transparently. 

For example, a simple deployment of a CrewAI‑based chatbot can be launched via a Kubernetes Deployment; the operator will detect and instrument all LLM and agent calls as soon as the pod starts. The instrumentation captures the sequence of agent steps, tool invocations, and model responses, along with latency and token metrics.

Step 6: Visualize metrics and traces in Grafana

With your pods instrumented and telemetry flowing to Grafana Cloud, open the AI Observability dashboards. 

The GenAI observability dashboard shows request rates, p95/p99 latencies, and cost metrics across different providers. The GenAI observability dashboard surfaces agent workflows, step durations, and tool success rates. The Vector DB and MCP dashboards provide context on database queries and protocol health. 

Because OpenLIT’s traces include LLM costs and token counts, Grafana can also estimate costs and highlight expensive calls. In the dashboard, you’ll see a service overview, individual traces for HTTP requests and OpenAI API calls, detailed spans with token usage, performance metrics (response times, error rates, throughput), and cost tracking.

Grafana’s alerting engine can trigger notifications when latency spikes, error rates increase, or token usage exceeds budget. Since the telemetry is OpenTelemetry‑native, you can build custom panels and alerts on top of Prometheus metrics and Tempo traces.

Next steps

You can also learn more about Grafana Cloud AI Observability in the official docs, including setup instructions and dashboards. You can also check out the first post in this series to see a full demo to better understand how to monitor AI workloads in Grafana Cloud, or check out our other AI blogs, including posts about our own LLM: Grafana Assistant.  of setting up a or check out our other blog posts

Taken collectively, these resources will help you move from a basic demo to a production-ready setup for your AI applications.

】

注意：由于人工智能，我们周围的世界正在发生变化。如今，任何人都可以成为开发人员，使用 LLM 来创建由 LLM 驱动的应用程序，然后用户可以使用更多的 LLM 与其进行交互。 

可观察性从业者需要适应，他们需要合适的工具来完成工作。在本系列中，我们将向您展示如何使用 Grafana Cloud 监控 AI 应用程序，包括生产中的工作负载、AI 代理、MCP服务器和零代码法学硕士（本文）。

使用大型语言模型和代理框架构建 AI 服务通常意味着在 Kubernetes 上运行复杂的微服务。可观察性至关重要，但对分布式系统中的每个 Pod 进行检测可能很快就会成为维护的噩梦。 

OpenLIT Operator 通过自动将 OpenTelemetry 检测注入您的 AI 工作负载来解决此问题，无需更改代码或重建映像。与 Grafana Cloud 中的 AI Observability 结合使用时，您可以在几分钟内监控整个集群的成本、延迟、令牌使用情况和代理工作流程。

在我们的 AI Observability 系列的最后一篇文章中，我们将向您展示如何通过结合 OpenLIT Operator 和 Grafana Cloud 来实现零代码，轻松入门AI 工作负载的可观察性。

为什么零代码检测很重要

传统的可观察性依赖于开发人员将检测库添加到其应用程序代码中。但在快速发展的生成式 AI 世界中，您的堆栈可能包括多个模型提供程序、代理框架、矢量数据库和自定义工具。使所有这些组件的仪器保持最新是一种负担。 

OpenLIT Operator 为 Kubernetes 带来了零代码 AI 可观察性。它会自动将 OpenTelemetry 检测注入并配置到您的 Pod 中，从而生成分布式跟踪和指标，而无需更改任何代码。由于它是基于 OpenTelemetry 标准构建的，因此它与现有的可观测性基础设施集成，并允许您在提供程序（OpenLIT、OpenInference、OpenLLMetry、自定义）之间切换，而无需重新部署应用程序。

这种零代码方法专为 AI 工作负载而设计。它为 Kubernetes 中运行的 LLM、矢量数据库和代理框架提供无缝可观察性。您可以跟踪令牌使用情况、监控代理工作流程、测量响应时间以及调试 AI 框架交互，所有这些都无需接触您的代码。

Grafana Cloud 中零代码可观察性的好处

您应该在 Grafana Cloud 中使用零代码可观察性的好处还有很多。

  • 快速入门：部署 OpenLIT Operator 一次，无需修改一行代码即可检测所有 AI 工作负载。
  • 全面覆盖：该算子支持主要的 LLM 提供商、向量数据库和代理框架，并可通过其插件架构扩展到其他提供商。
  • 供应商中立性：基于 OpenTelemetry 构建，该算子允许您将遥测数据发送到Grafana Cloud、自托管 OpenTelemetry 收集器或任何 OTLP 兼容后端。
  • 成本和性能洞察：分布式跟踪捕获令牌使用情况、成本、延迟和代理步骤序列，使您能够优化模型选择和资源分配。

如何在 Grafana Cloud 中为 AI 应用程序设置零代码可观察性

现在我们已经介绍了您应该使用的原因Grafana Cloud 实现了零代码可观察性，让我们看看如何实现这一目标，从工作流程的高级说明开始，然后是快速入门的分步说明。

如果您在任何地方遇到困难或需要自己设置方面的帮助，请单击 Grafana Cloud UI 右上角的 pulsar 图标，打开与 Grafana Assistant，我们专门构建的 LLM，可以帮助解决事件、管理仪表板和回答产品问题。

架构概述

LLM 和代理等 AI 应用程序在 Kubernetes 集群的 Pod 内运行。 OpenLIT Operator 持续监控这些 pod，并根据您的检测策略检查它们。当它找到匹配的 pod 时，它会自动注入一个 init 容器来设置 OpenTelemetry 工具，从而无需手动更改应用程序代码即可实现可观察性。

遥测数据将发送到 OpenLIT 收集器或直接发送到 Grafana Cloud 的 OpenTelemetry 协议 (OTLP) 网关。然后，Grafana Cloud 中的 AI 可观测性仪表板可视化延迟、成本和质量指标。

工作流程由四个关键部分组成：

  1. AI 工作负载：运行 LLM、向量数据库或代理框架（例如 LangChain、CrewAI 或 OpenAI Agents）的 Pod。该运算符支持各种 LLM 提供商（OpenAI、Anthropic、Google、AWS Bedrock、Mistral）和框架（LangChain、LlamaIndex、CrewAI、Haystack、DSPy 等）。
  2. OpenLIT Operator：一个 Kubernetes 运算符，可根据标签选择器将 OpenTelemetry 检测注入到选定的 pod 中。该运算符是 OpenTelemetry 原生的，允许您在不更改应用程序代码的情况下切换提供程序。
  3. OpenLIT 收集器：从已检测的 Pod 收集跟踪和指标。您可以通过 Helm 在集群中运行它，或将遥测数据直接发送到 Grafana Cloud 的 OTLP 端点。
  4. Grafana Cloud：将跟踪存储在 Tempo 中，将指标存储在 Prometheus 通过我们完全托管的平台。我们的 AI 可观测性解决方案为 GenAI、向量数据库、代理和模型上下文协议 (MCP) 提供预构建的仪表板，让您能够探索延迟百分位、令牌和成本指标、代理步骤序列和评估结果。

第 1 步：添加 AI 可观测性集成

在检测集群之前，将 AI 可观测性集成添加到 Grafana Cloud 堆栈中。这可以通过点击左侧菜单中的连接并按照我们文档中概述的步骤来完成。

这将配置仪表板并设置托管用于接收您的跟踪和指标的 OTLP 网关。遥测数据到达后，仪表板会自动填充请求率、延迟分布和成本摘要。

第 2 步：准备 Kubernetes 环境

要遵循本指南，您需要一个具有集群管理权限、Helm 和配置的 kubectl 的 Kubernetes 集群。如果您没有集群，可以使用 k3d 或 minikube 在本地创建一个集群。要进行快速测试，请使用以下命令创建集群：

k3d cluster create openlit-demo

第 3 步：部署 OpenLIT Operator

首先添加 OpenLIT Helm 存储库并更新图表：

helm repo add openlit https://openlit.github.io/helm/
舵回购更新

安装 OpenLIT Operator 以启用零代码检测：

helm install openlit-operator openlit/openlit-operator

验证 Operator pod 是否正在运行：

kubectl get pods -n openlit -l app.kubernetes.io/name=openlit-operator

您应该看到操作符处于正在运行状态。

第 4 步：创建 AutoInstrumentation 资源

AutoInstrumentation 自定义资源定义要检测的 pod 以及如何配置注入的检测。它指定用于定位 AI 应用程序的标签选择器、检测提供程序（默认为 OpenLIT）以及用于发送遥测数据的 OTLP 端点。

以下是一个最小示例，用于检测标记为 instrumentation=openlit 的 Pod 并将数据发送到 Grafana Cloud：

apiVersion: openlit.io/v1alpha1
种类：自动仪表
元数据：
名称：grafana-可观测性
命名空间：默认
规格：
选择器：
匹配标签：
仪表：开放式
python：
仪器仪表：
启用：真
奥特普：
端点：“https://otlp-gateway-.grafana.net/otlp”# Grafana OTLP网关
标题：
Authorization: "Basic " # 替换为base64编码的instanceID:token
资源：
属性：
部署.环境：“生产”
service.namespace: "ai-services"

应用清单：

kubectl apply -f autoinstrumentation.yaml

已经有人工智能应用程序在运行？重新启动与您的选择器匹配的 Pod 以获取注入的工具：

kubectl rollout restart deployment your-deployment-name

当 Pod 重新启动时，OpenLIT Operator 会自动注入一个配置 Python 检测的 init 容器。 Pod 开始发出包含 LLM 成本、令牌使用情况和代理性能指标的分布式跟踪。

第 5 步：部署您的 AI 应用程序（无需更改代码）

您现在可以正常部署或继续运行您的 AI 工作负载。无论您使用的是 OpenAI Agents SDK、CrewAI、LangChain 还是自定义 Python 服务，您都不需要修改代码。运营商识别支持的框架和模型提供商，并透明地对它们进行检测。 

例如，可以通过 Kubernetes Deployment 启动基于 CrewAI 的聊天机器人的简单部署； Pod 启动后，操作员将立即检测并检测所有 LLM 和代理呼叫。该检测捕获代理步骤、工具调用和模型响应的序列，以及延迟和令牌指标。

第 6 步：在 Grafana 中可视化指标和跟踪

对 Pod 进行检测并将遥测数据流向 Grafana Cloud 后，打开 AI Observability 仪表板。 

GenAI 可观测性仪表板显示不同提供商的请求率、p95/p99 延迟和成本指标。 GenAI 可观测性仪表板显示代理工作流程、步骤持续时间和工具成功率。 Vector DB 和 MCP 仪表板提供有关数据库查询和协议运行状况的上下文。 

由于 OpenLIT 的跟踪包括 LLM 成本和代币计数，因此 Grafana 还可以估算成本并突出显示昂贵的调用。在仪表板中，您将看到服务概述、HTTP 请求和 OpenAI API 调用的单独跟踪、令牌使用的详细范围、性能指标（响应时间、错误率、吞吐量）和成本跟踪。

Grafana 的警报引擎可以在延迟峰值、错误率增加或令牌使用超出预算时触发通知。由于遥测是 OpenTelemetry 原生的，因此您可以在 Prometheus 指标和 Tempo 跟踪之上构建自定义面板和警报。

后续步骤

您还可以在官方文档，包括设置说明离子和仪表板。您还可以查看本系列的第一篇文章，查看完整的演示，以便更好地了解如何监控 Grafana Cloud 中的 AI 工作负载，或者查看我们的其他 AI 博客，包括有关我们自己的帖子法学硕士：Grafana 助理。  设置或查看我们的其他博客文章

总的来说，这些资源将帮助您从基本演示转变为 AI 应用程序的生产就绪设置。

item.title: 【Observe your AI agents: End‑to‑end tracing with OpenLIT and Grafana Cloud】观察您的 AI 代理：使用 OpenLIT 和 Grafana Cloud 进行端到端跟踪
item.description:
【

Note: The world is changing all around us thanks to AI. Today, anyone and everyone can be a developer, using LLMs to create LLM-powered applications, which users can then interact with by using even more LLMs. 

Observability practitioners need to adapt and they need the right tools for the job. In this series, we'll show you how to use Grafana Cloud to monitor AI applications, including workloads in production, AI agents (this post), MCP servers, and zero-code LLMs.

In another post in this series, we discussed how to instrument large language model (LLM) calls. This can be a good starting point, but generative AI workloads increasingly rely on agents, which are systems that plan, call tools, reason, and act autonomously. 

And their non‑deterministic behavior makes incidents harder to diagnose, in part, because the same prompt can trigger different tool sequences and costs. 

AI agents combine LLM reasoning with external tools and dynamic workflows, and observability data must serve as a feedback loop for continuous improvement. Without proper tracing, you end up guessing why an agent took a particular path. 

In this guide, you'll learn how to use the OpenLIT SDK to capture agent‑level telemetry and how to use Grafana Cloud to visualize every step.

Why observability matters for agents

Traditional APM covers infrastructure metrics and latency, but that's not enough to get a holistic view of your agents. AI Observability in Grafana Cloud uses the OpenLIT SDK to automatically generate distributed traces and metrics to provide insights into each agentic event. 

AI Observability provides five prebuilt dashboards that analyze response times, error rates, throughput, token usage, and costs across your AI stack. Beyond raw metrics, OpenLIT captures agent names, actions, tool calls, token usage, and errors. This enables:

  • Full sequence visibility: Follow a request from the user query through planning, tool invocations, LLM calls, and final responses. Each span in the trace shows the prompt, selected tool, and reasoning chain.
  • Cost and token tracking: For each step, you see token counts and API costs, so you can optimize tool choices and model selection.
  • Behavioral troubleshooting: Agent traces reveal reasoning paths and tool usage. If the agent produces an incorrect answer, you can reconstruct the chain to find where it went wrong.
  • Unified dashboards and alerting: Grafana Cloud combines fully managed versions of Prometheus, Tempo, and Loki to present metrics, traces, and logs in one place, with optional alerts on cost thresholds or latency spikes.

Benefits of agent observability in Grafana Cloud

Agent observability is more than just infrastructure monitoring. With OpenLIT and Grafana Cloud, you gain:

  • Predictable costs: Identify which agent step or tool call accounts for most of your spending and reroute simple queries to cheaper models.
  • Performance optimization: Detect latency spikes at specific stages (e.g., search API vs. LLM) and adjust concurrency or caching accordingly.
  • Quality assurance: Traces can be replayed to understand reasoning mistakes, while integrated evaluation tools in OpenLIT (such as hallucination detection and toxicity analysis) provide safety metrics.
  • Faster debugging: When an agent fails, you have a single trace that links user input, internal reasoning, external calls, and the error, making root‑cause analysis straightforward.
  • Future‑proof instrumentation: OpenTelemetry semantic conventions for AI agents are evolving; by using OpenLIT, you adopt these standards and avoid vendor lock‑in. Grafana Cloud’s integration ensures your telemetry remains compatible as conventions mature.

How to monitor your AI agents with Grafana Cloud

Now that you understand some of the nuances of observing AI agents, let's show you how you can use prebuilt capabilities in Grafana Cloud to start collecting and visualizing telemetry from your agents.

And if you get stuck anywhere along the way or need help with your own setup, click on the pulsar icon in the top-right corner of the Grafana Cloud UI to open a chat with Grafana Assistant, our purpose-built LLM that can help troubleshoot incidents, manage dashboards, and answer product questions.

Architecture overview

AI agents orchestrate multiple actions: planning, calling external tools or models, and producing a response. OpenLIT instruments each of these steps and emits OpenTelemetry spans and metrics. You can send this data directly to Grafana Cloud or via an OpenTelemetry Collector. The following diagram shows how a user request flows through an agent orchestrator and is monitored:

The workflow consists of four key pieces:

  1. User query: A customer sends a message to your agent.
  2. Agent orchestrator: Frameworks like CrewAI or the OpenAI Agents SDK break the task into sequential steps: plan, call a tool (e.g., a search API), call an LLM, and generate a result.
  3. OpenLIT instrumentation: A single openlit.init() call instruments the entire agent pipeline. Each planning step, tool call, and model completion is captured as an OpenTelemetry span.
  4. Grafana Cloud: Metrics and traces flow into Grafana Cloud’s managed Prometheus and Tempo backends, where pre‑built AI dashboards visualize performance and costs.

Step 1: Install the AI Observability integration

Start by adding AI Observability to your Grafana Cloud stack. This can be done by clicking on Connections in the left-side menu and following the steps outlined in our documentation.

This installs the five dashboards mentioned earlier (GenAI observability, GenAI evaluations, vector DB observability, MCP observability, and GPU monitoring). When metrics arrive, these dashboards automatically populate with latency histograms, token counts, cost summaries, and evaluation results.

Step 2: Install OpenLIT

OpenLIT is an OpenTelemetry‑native SDK for instrumenting GenAI workloads. Install it alongside your agent framework:

pip install openlit crewai

OpenLIT supports dozens of frameworks, including CrewAI, OpenAI Agents, LangChain, AutoGen, and others. The SDK automatically instruments supported libraries; no manual span creation is required.

Step 3: Instrument your agent

OpenLIT can be added with a single line of code. Below is an example that uses CrewAI to build a simple agent with two tools: a search tool and a summarizer. 

The agent plans its steps, uses the search tool to fetch content, and then summarizes the result. OpenLIT records each step, tool call, and model completion. You can swap CrewAI with the OpenAI Agents SDK—the instrumentation code remains the same.

import os
import openlit # Instruments all supported frameworks when initialised
from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, agent_tools # CrewAI framework
from your_search_module import SearchTool # hypothetical search tool
from your_summarise_module import SummariseTool # hypothetical summariser
openlit.init() # one line to enable OpenTelemetry tracing and metrics
# Define tools the agent can use
search_tool = SearchTool()
summarise_tool = SummariseTool()
# Compose an agent with a planning function and tool access
assistant = Agent(
name="research_assistant",
role="Find relevant sources and summarise them",
tools=[search_tool, summarise_tool],
planning=True # enable internal reasoning and tool selection
)
# Define a task for the agent
task = Task(
description="Provide a concise summary of the latest developments in battery recycling.",
expected_output="A two‑paragraph summary highlighting key advances",
)
# Create a crew to execute the task
crew = Crew(
agents=[assistant],
tasks=[task],
verbose=True,
)
if __name__ == "__main__":
result = crew.execute()
print(result)

When this script runs, OpenLIT automatically captures:

  • LLM prompts and completions: The prompts sent to the LLM and the responses returned
  • Token usage and costs: Counts the tokens for each call and estimates API cost
  • Agent names and actions: Identifies which agent or sub‑agent executed each step
  • Tool usage: Records which tool was invoked and its parameters
  • Errors: Surfaces exceptions such as API failures or tool errors

This information becomes distributed spans in Tempo and metrics in Prometheus. If you use the OpenAI Agents SDK, the pattern is the same: Call openlit.init() before constructing your agent, and every agent step will emit telemetry.

Step 4: Forward telemetry to Grafana Cloud

To send traces and metrics directly to Grafana Cloud, set the following environment variables before running your agent. Replace the values with your own service name, environment, and Grafana credentials:

# identify your service and environment
export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="agent-demo"
export OTEL_DEPLOYMENT_ENVIRONMENT="production"
# Grafana Cloud OTLP endpoint
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://otlp-gateway-.grafana.net/otlp"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="Authorization=Basic "
# Set any API keys for your agent framework
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
python agent_service.py

Step 5: Explore your agent traces and metrics

With your agent running, open Grafana Cloud. Navigate to AI Observability and select the AI Agents dashboard. Here you can:

  • View complete traces: Each user request produces a trace containing spans for planning, tool invocations, LLM calls, and response generation. The traces page in OpenLIT provides detailed span analysis and execution flow, and Grafana Cloud mirrors this via Tempo.
  • Monitor metrics and costs: Custom dashboards can display throughput, latency, token usage, and cost metrics stored in Prometheus.
  • Filter and investigate errors: The errors page surfaces traces with exceptions and allows filtering by time range or exception type.
  • Correlate with infrastructure: Grafana Cloud unifies metrics, logs, and traces, so you can correlate an agent’s slow step with CPU spikes or external API rate limits.

Grafana Cloud’s AI dashboards are purpose-built for GenAI applications and include separate panels for LLM performance, agent performance, vector database operations, and GPU health. Because OpenLIT uses OpenTelemetry standards, you can extend these dashboards or forward the data to other observability tools if required.

Next steps

Want to go further? In the next blog in this series, we’ll show, step by step, how to enable this step by step for an MCP client.

You can also learn more about Grafana Cloud AI Observability in the official docs, including setup instructions and dashboards. These resources will help you move from a basic demo to a production-ready setup for your AI applications.

】

注意：由于人工智能，我们周围的世界正在发生变化。如今，任何人都可以成为开发人员，使用 LLM 来创建由 LLM 驱动的应用程序，然后用户可以使用更多的 LLM 与其进行交互。 

可观察性从业者需要适应，他们需要合适的工具来完成工作。在本系列中，我们将向您展示如何使用 Grafana Cloud 监控 AI 应用程序，包括生产中的工作负载、AI 代理（本文）、MCP 服务器和零代码 LLM。

在本系列的另一篇文章中，我们讨论了如何检测大型语言模型 (LLM) 调用。这可能是一个很好的起点，但生成式人工智能工作负载越来越依赖于代理，它们是自主规划、调用工具、推理和行动的系统。 

它们的不确定性行为使得事件更难以诊断，部分原因是相同的提示可能会触发不同的工具序列和成本。 

AI 代理将 LLM 推理与外部工具和动态工作流程相结合，可观测数据必须作为持续改进的反馈循环。如果没有适当的跟踪，您最终只能猜测代理为何选择特定路径。 

在本指南中，您将了解如何使用 OpenLIT SDK 捕获代理级遥测数据，以及如何使用 Grafana Cloud 可视化每个步骤。

为什么可观察性对代理很重要

传统 APM 涵盖基础设施指标和延迟，但这不足以全面了解代理。 Grafana Cloud 中的AI 可观测性使用 OpenLIT SDK 自动生成分布式跟踪和指标，以提供对每个代理事件的见解。 

AI Observability 提供五个预构建的仪表板，用于分析整个 AI 堆栈的响应时间、错误率、吞吐量、令牌使用情况和成本。除了原始指标之外，OpenLIT 还捕获代理名称、操作、工具调用、令牌使用情况和错误。这可以实现：

  • 完整的序列可见性：通过规划、工具调用、LLM 调用和最终响应来遵循用户查询的请求。跟踪中的每个跨度都会显示提示、所选工具和推理链。
  • 成本和令牌跟踪：对于每个步骤，您都会看到令牌计数和 API 成本，以便您可以优化工具选择和模型选择。
  • 行为故障排除：代理跟踪揭示推理路径和工具使用情况。如果代理产生了错误的答案，您可以重建链以查找出错的地方。
  • 统一的仪表板和警报：Grafana Cloud 结合了Prometheus 的完全托管版本、Tempo 和 Loki 在一个位置呈现指标、跟踪和日志，并带有可选警报成本阈值或延迟峰值。

Grafana Cloud 中代理可观察性的好处

代理可观察性不仅仅是基础设施监控。借助 OpenLIT 和 Grafana Cloud，您可以获得：

  • 可预测的成本：确定哪个代理步骤或工具调用占了您的大部分支出，并将简单查询重新路由到更便宜的模型。
  • 性能优化：检测特定阶段的延迟峰值（例如，搜索 API 与 LLM）并相应地调整并发或缓存。
  • 质量保证：可以重放跟踪以了解推理错误，而 OpenLIT 中的集成评估工具（例如幻觉检测和毒性分析）提供安全指标。
  • 更快的调试：当代理失败时，您有一个链接用户输入、内部推理、外部调用和错误的跟踪，使根本原因分析变得简单。
  • 面向未来的仪器：OpenTelemetry 语义约定人工智能代理正在不断发展；通过使用 OpenLIT，您可以采用这些标准并避免供应商锁定。 Grafana Cloud 的集成可确保您的遥测数据在约定成熟时保持兼容。

如何使用 Grafana Cloud 监控您的 AI 代理

现在您已经了解了观察 AI 代理的一些细微差别，让我们向您展示如何使用 Grafana Cloud 中的预构建功能来开始收集和可视化来自代理的遥测数据。

如果您在此过程中遇到困难或需要有关自己设置的帮助，请单击Grafana Cloud UI 右上角的 pulsar 图标可打开与 Grafana Assistant 的聊天，这是我们专门构建的 LLM，可以帮助解决事件、管理仪表板和回答产品问题。

架构概述

人工智能代理协调多个操作：规划、调用外部工具或模型以及生成响应。 OpenLIT 检测每个步骤并发出 OpenTelemetry 范围和指标。您可以将此数据直接发送到 Grafana Cloud 或通过 OpenTelemetry Collector。下图显示了用户请求如何流经代理协调器并受到监控：

工作流程由四个关键部分组成：

  1. 用户查询：客户向您的代理发送消息。
  2. 代理协调器：框架像 CrewAI 或 OpenAI Agents SDK 这样的工具将任务分解为连续的步骤：计划、调用工具（例如搜索 API）、调用 LLM 并生成结果。
  3. OpenLIT 检测：单个 openlit.init() 调用可检测整个代理管道。每个规划步骤、工具调用和模型完成都会作为 OpenTelemetry 范围进行捕获。
  4. Grafana Cloud：指标和跟踪流入 Grafana Cloud 的托管 Prometheus 和 Tempo 后端，其中预构建的 AI 仪表板可可视化性能和成本。

第 1 步：安装 AI Observability 集成

首先将 AI Observability 添加到 Grafana云栈。这可以通过点击左侧菜单中的连接并按照我们文档中列出的步骤来完成。

这将安装前面提到的五个仪表板（GenAI 可观测性、GenAI 评估、矢量 DB 可观测性、MCP 可观测性和 GPU 监控）。当指标到达时，这些仪表板会自动填充延迟直方图、令牌计数、成本摘要和评估结果。

第 2 步：安装 OpenLIT

OpenLIT 是用于检测 GenAI 工作负载的 OpenTelemetry 原生 SDK。将其与代理框架一起安装：

pip install openlit craftai

OpenLIT 支持数十种框架，包括 CrewAI、OpenAI Agents、LangChain、AutoGen 等。 SDK 自动检测支持的库；不需要手动创建跨度。

步骤 3：检测您的代理

可以使用一行代码添加 OpenLIT。下面是一个使用 CrewAI 构建一个简单代理的示例，其中包含两个工具：搜索工具和摘要器。 

代理计划其步骤，使用搜索工具获取内容，然后总结结果。 OpenLIT 记录每个步骤、工具调用和模型完成情况。您可以将 CrewAI 与 OpenAI Agents SDK 交换 — 检测代码保持不变。

import os
import openlit # 初始化时检测所有支持的框架
fromcrewai import Agent, Task, Crew, agent_tools # CrewAI 框架
from your_search_module import SearchTool # 假设的搜索工具
from your_summarise_module import SummariseTool # 假设的摘要器
openlit.init() # 一行启用 OpenTelemetry 跟踪和指标
# 定义代理可以使用的工具
搜索工具 = 搜索工具()
summarise_tool = SummariseTool()
# 构建一个具有规划功能和工具访问权限的代理
助理 = 代理人(
名称=“研究助理”，
role="查找相关来源并进行总结",
工具=[搜索工具，总结工具]，
Planning=True # 启用内部推理和工具选择
）
# 为代理定义一个任务
任务 = 任务（
description="提供最新开发的简明摘要电池回收方面的评论。",
Expected_output="两段式总结，强调关键进展",
）
# 创建一个crew来执行任务
船员=船员（
特工=[助理],
任务=[任务],
详细=真，
）
如果 __name__ == "__main__":
结果=船员.execute()
打印（结果）

当此脚本运行时，OpenLIT 会自动捕获：

  • LLM 提示和完成：发送给 LLM 的提示和返回的响应
  • 令牌使用和成本：计算每次调用的令牌并估计 API 成本
  • 代理名称和操作：标识执行每个调用的代理或子代理步骤
  • 工具使用：记录调用了哪个工具及其参数
  • 错误：显示 API 故障或工具错误等异常

此信息成为 Prometheus 中 Tempo 和指标中的分布式跨度。如果您使用 OpenAI Agents SDK，模式是相同的：在构建代理之前调用 openlit.init()，每个代理步骤都会发出遥测数据。

第 4 步：将遥测数据转发到 Grafana Cloud

要将跟踪和指标直接发送到 Grafana Cloud，请在运行代理之前设置以下环境变量。将这些值替换为您自己的服务名称、环境和 Grafana 凭据：

# 标识您的服务和环境
导出 OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="代理演示"
导出 OTEL_DEPLOYMENT_ENVIRONMENT="生产"
# Grafana Cloud OTLP 端点
导出 OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://otlp-gateway-<区域>.grafana.net/otlp"
导出 OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="授权=基本 "
# 为您的代理框架设置任何 API 密钥
导出 OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
python 代理服务.py

第 5 步：探索代理跟踪和指标

代理运行时，打开 Grafana Cloud。导航到 AI Observability 并选择 AI Agents 仪表板。您可以在此处：

  • 查看完整跟踪：每个用户请求都会生成一个跟踪，其中包含规划、工具调用、LLM 调用和响应生成的范围。 OpenLIT 中的跟踪页面提供详细的跨度分析和执行流程，Grafana Cloud 通过 Tempo 对此进行镜像。
  • 监控指标和成本：自定义仪表板可以显示 Prometheus 中存储的吞吐量、延迟、令牌使用情况和成本指标。
  • 过滤和调查错误：错误页面显示有异常的跟踪，并允许按时间范围或异常进行过滤
  • 与基础设施关联：Grafana Cloud 统一了指标、日志和跟踪，因此您可以将代理的缓慢步骤与 CPU 峰值或外部 API 速率限制关联起来。

Grafana Cloud 的 AI 仪表板专为 GenAI 应用程序构建，包括用于 LLM 性能、代理性能、矢量数据库操作、GPU 健康状况。由于 OpenLIT 使用 OpenTelemetry 标准，因此您可以根据需要扩展这些仪表板或将数据转发到其他可观测性工具。

后续步骤

想要更进一步吗？在本系列的下一篇博客中，我们将逐步展示如何为 MCP 客户端启用此功能。

您还可以在 官方文档，包括设置说明和仪表板。这些资源将帮助您从基本演示过渡到 AI 应用程序的生产就绪设置。

item.title: 【Monitor Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers with OpenLIT and Grafana Cloud】使用 OpenLIT 和 Grafana Cloud 监控模型上下文协议 (MCP) 服务器
item.description:
【

Note: The world is changing all around us thanks to AI. Today, anyone and everyone can be a developer, using LLMs to create LLM-powered applications, which users can then interact with by using even more LLMs. 

Observability practitioners need to adapt and they need the right tools for the job. In this series, we'll show you how to use Grafana Cloud to monitor AI applications, including workloads in production, AI agents, MCP servers (this post), and zero-code LLMs.

Large language models don’t work in a vacuum. They often rely on Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers to fetch additional context from external tools or data sources. 

MCP provides a standard way for AI agents to talk to tool servers, but this extra layer introduces complexity. Without visibility, an MCP server becomes a black box: you send a request and hope a tool answers. When something breaks, it’s hard to tell if the agent, the server or the downstream API failed. 

In this guide, you'll learn how to instrument MCP servers using OpenLIT and how to analyze those servers in Grafana Cloud.

Why MCP observability matters

In an agentic system, an MCP server may route tool calls to multiple services. Observability helps you answer critical questions about:

  • Latency spikes: When a tool is slow to respond, user experience suffers. By examining request throughput and the 95th/99th percentile latency distributions, you can determine whether a downstream API or the MCP layer is responsible. 
  • Silent failures: For example, a tool returning partial data or timing out often goes unnoticed without structured telemetry. End‑to‑end tracing across the agent, MCP server, and external tools provides the full context needed to diagnose these issues. 
  • Cross‑service visibility: This is important because MCP calls cross-network and language boundaries. For example, OpenTelemetry propagates context, so spans started in a Python client link seamlessly to spans in a Node.js tool server, producing a coherent trace across systems.
  • Context window usage: Resource consumption can grow quickly as agents query more tools. By tracking context window usage and memory consumption, you can right‑size your MCP servers and avoid over‑allocating resources.

AI Observability in Grafana Cloud supports MCP out of the box. The solution includes pre‑built dashboards for tool performance, protocol health, resource usage, and error tracking.

Benefits of MCP observability in Grafana Cloud

Observing your MCP servers unlocks a range of advantages:

  • End‑to‑end tracing shows the entire path of a request—from the agent through the MCP server to each tool call—so you can pinpoint bottlenecks and failures. 
  • Detailed performance metrics like tool_invocation_duration_ms and invocation counts, help you identify slow or overused tools and adjust resource allocation accordingly. 
  • Scalability and cost control are enabled through context‑window and memory usage telemetry, so you can right‑size servers and avoid over‑provisioning. Because OpenTelemetry uses an open, vendor‑neutral format, your instrumentation remains portable; you can route data to Grafana, a self‑hosted OTLP stack or any other backend without code changes. 
  • Security and compliance is also available with MCP monitoring by auditing tool interactions and ensuring protocols are used as intended.

How to monitor your MCP server with Grafana Cloud

Next, let's take a high-level look at how you can use Grafana Cloud to observe your MCP server, then we'll walk through the setup process so you can get up and running today.

And if you get stuck anywhere along the way or need help with your own setup, click on the pulsar icon in the top-right corner of the Grafana Cloud UI to open a chat with Grafana Assistant, our purpose-built LLM that can help troubleshoot incidents, manage dashboards, and answer product questions.

Architecture overview

The diagram below illustrates how agent interactions with an MCP server are instrumented and visualized. 

The agent or client calls the MCP server to execute tools. OpenLIT instruments both the client and the server, capturing spans for context management, tool selection, and tool execution. These traces and metrics are exported to Grafana Cloud, where pre‑built dashboards provide insight into performance and failures.

The workflow consists of five key components:

  1. Agent/client: AI agents use the MCP protocol to invoke tools hosted on external servers.
  2. MCP server: Hosts one or more tools (e.g., search, database query). The server handles context loading, manages tool state, and responds to requests.
  3. External tools: Actual services (databases, APIs) that do the work. They may be local or remote.
  4. OpenLIT instrumentation: A single openlit.init() call instruments on both the client and server; context interactions, and tool executions generate OpenTelemetry spans.
  5. Grafana Cloud: Collected traces and metrics flow into Grafana Cloud’s fully managed Prometheus and Tempo backends, where specialized MCP dashboards offer visibility into protocol usage.

Step 1: Install the AI Observability solution

Start by adding the AI Observability integration to your Grafana Cloud stack. This can be done by clicking on Connections in the left-side menu and following the remaining steps outlined in our documentation.

This provision pre‑built dashboards, including one for MCP observability, and configured a managed OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) gateway to receive traces and metrics. Once telemetry flows in, the dashboards automatically populate with call rates, latency percentiles, and error counts.

Step 2: Install OpenLIT and the MCP library

OpenLIT provides auto‑instrumentation for MCP alongside LLMs, vector stores, and agent frameworks. Install OpenLIT and the mcp library (which implements the client and server) via pip:

pip install openlit mcp

After installation, a single call to openlit.init() automatically instruments all MCP operations. If you choose to run your own telemetry collector instead of Grafana’s OTLP gateway, OpenLIT can be self‑hosted via Docker Compose or deployed to Kubernetes using the OpenLIT Operator.

Step 3: Instrument your MCP application

Instrumentation requires just two lines of code. Below is a simple example of an MCP server that exposes a search_documents tool. OpenLIT instruments the server, capturing each tool invocation and context interaction:

import openlit
from mcp import Server
openlit.init() # enable OpenTelemetry tracing and metrics
# Create an MCP server instance
server = Server()
# Define a tool to fetch documents
@server.tool("search_documents")
def search_documents(query: str):
# Imagine this function calls a search API or database
results = document_search(query)
return results
# Run the MCP server on localhost
server.run(host="localhost", port=8080)
# When a client invokes search_documents, OpenLIT captures:
# * Context protocol interactions (e.g., context loading and management)
# * Tool usage metrics (latency and success rate)
# * Protocol handshake performance
# * Resource usage (context window size, memory)
# * Errors or exceptions:contentReference[oaicite:14]{index=14}

To instrument an MCP client, use the Client class from the mcp library and call openlit.init() before making requests:

import openlit
from mcp import Client
openlit.init()
client = Client("http://localhost:8080")
tools = client.list_tools() # Lists available tools
result = client.call_tool(
"search_documents",
{"query": "AI observability"}
) # Invokes the tool
# All client operations are automatically instrumented:contentReference[oaicite:15]{index=15}

OpenLIT supports zero‑code instrumentation via a CLI wrapper. To instrument an existing MCP service without code modifications, use:

openlit-instrument \
--service-name my-mcp-app \
python your_mcp_app.py
# With custom settings:
openlit-instrument \
--otlp-endpoint http://127.0.0.1:4318 \
--service-name my-mcp-app \
--environment production \
python your_mcp_app.py

Step 4: Configure environment variables

To send your traces and metrics to Grafana Cloud, you need OpenTelemetry credentials. Generate them from the Grafana Cloud portal and set the following variables in your environment:

export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://otlp-gateway-.grafana.net/otlp"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="Authorization=Basic "
export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="mcp-server-demo"
export OTEL_DEPLOYMENT_ENVIRONMENT="production"

When you run your client or server with these variables, the OpenLIT SDK automatically sends telemetry data to Grafana Cloud.

Step 5: Explore the MCP observability dashboard

After you start your instrumented MCP client and server, open Grafana Cloud and navigate to AI Observability → MCP Observability. The dashboard provides:

  • Tool performance: Call latency histograms, success rates, and invocation counts per tool.
  • Protocol health: Session stability and connection metrics to detect handshake issues.
  • Resource usage: Context window size, memory, and data access patterns, helping you optimize server resources.
  • Error tracking: Lists failed operations with trace IDs and detailed exception information to aid debugging

You can build custom dashboards by querying Prometheus metrics (e.g., tool invocation duration) and Tempo traces. Because OpenLIT uses OpenTelemetry, you’re not locked into a single backend. You can forward telemetry to any OTLP‑compatible observability stack.

Next steps

Ready to learn more? In the final blog in this series, we’ll show how to set this up, step by step, for a zero-code instrumentation approach to AI Observability.

You can also learn more about Grafana Cloud AI Observability in the official docs, including setup instructions and dashboards. These resources will help you move from a basic demo to a production-ready setup for your AI applications.

】

注意：由于人工智能，我们周围的世界正在发生变化。如今，任何人都可以成为开发人员，使用 LLM 来创建由 LLM 驱动的应用程序，然后用户可以使用更多的 LLM 与其进行交互。 

可观察性从业者需要适应，他们需要合适的工具来完成工作。在本系列中，我们将向您展示如何使用 Grafana Cloud 监控 AI 应用程序，包括生产中的工作负载、AI 代理、MCP 服务器（本文）和零代码法学硕士。

大型语言模型不能在真空中工作。他们通常依赖模型上下文协议（MCP）服务器从外部工具或数据源获取额外的上下文。 

MCP 为 AI 代理提供了一种与工具服务器对话的标准方式，但这个额外的层会带来复杂性。如果没有可见性，MCP 服务器就变成了一个黑匣子：您发送请求并希望工具能够回答。当出现故障时，很难判断代理、服务器还是下游 API 是否出现故障。 

在本指南中，您将了解如何使用 OpenLIT 检测 MCP 服务器以及如何在 Grafana Cloud 中分析这些服务器。

为什么 MCP 可观察性很重要

在代理系统中，MCP 服务器可以将工具调用路由到多个服务。可观察性可帮助您回答以下关键问题：

  • 延迟峰值：当工具响应缓慢时，用户体验会受到影响。通过检查请求吞吐量和第 95/99 个百分点的延迟分布，您可以确定是下游 API 还是 MCP 层负责。 
  • 无声故障：例如，如果没有结构化遥测，返回部分数据或超时的工具通常不会被注意到。跨代理、MCP 服务器和外部工具的端到端跟踪提供了诊断这些问题所需的完整上下文。 
  • 跨服务可见性：这很重要，因为 MCP 调用跨网络和语言边界。例如，OpenTelemetry 传播上下文，因此在 Python 客户端中启动的 Span 无缝链接到 Node.js 工具服务器中的 Span，从而在系统之间生成一致的跟踪。
  • 上下文窗口使用情况：随着代理查询更多工具，资源消耗会快速增长。通过跟踪上下文窗口使用情况和内存消耗，您可以调整 MCP 服务器的大小并避免过度分配资源。

Grafana Cloud 中的AI Observability 支持开箱即用的 MCP。该解决方案包括用于工具性能、协议运行状况、资源使用情况和错误跟踪的预构建仪表板。

Grafana Cloud 中 MCP 可观察性的优势

观察 MCP 服务器可带来一系列优势：

  • End‑端到端跟踪显示请求的整个路径（从代理通过 MCP 服务器到每个工具调用），以便您可以查明瓶颈和故障。 
  • 详细 性能指标（例如 tool_invocacy_duration_ms 和调用计数）可帮助您识别速度缓慢或过度使用的工具，并相应地调整资源分配。 
  • 可扩展性和成本控制通过上下文窗口和内存使用遥测实现，因此您可以调整服务器大小并避免过度配置。由于 OpenTelemetry 使用开放的、供应商中立的格式，因此您的仪器保持便携；您可以将数据路由到 Grafana、自托管 OTLP 堆栈或任何其他后端，而无需更改代码。 
  • 安全性和合规性还可以通过审核工具交互并确保按预期使用协议来实现 MCP 监控。

如何使用 Grafana Cloud 监控您的 MCP 服务器

接下来，让我们深入了解一下如何使用 Grafana Cloud 来观察您的 MCP 服务器，然后我们将逐步完成设置过程，以便您今天就可以开始运行。

并且如果 您在任何地方遇到困难或需要有关自己设置的帮助，请单击 Grafana Cloud UI 右上角的 pulsar 图标，打开与 Grafana Assistant 的聊天，这是我们专门构建的 LLM，可以帮助解决事件问题，管理仪表板，并回答产品问题。

架构概述

下图说明了如何对代理与 MCP 服务器的交互进行检测和可视化。 

代理或客户端调用MCP服务器来执行工具。 OpenLIT 检测客户端和服务器，捕获上下文管理、工具选择和工具执行的范围。这些跟踪和指标将导出到 Grafana Cloud，其中预构建的仪表板可提供对性能和故障的深入了解。

工作流程由五个关键组件组成：

  1. 代理/客户端：AI 代理使用 MCP 协议调用外部服务器上托管的工具。
  2. MCP 服务器：托管一个或多个工具（例如搜索、数据库查询）。服务器处理上下文加载、管理工具状态并响应请求。
  3. 外部工具：完成工作的实际服务（数据库、API）。它们可以是本地的或远程的。
  4. OpenLIT 工具：客户端和服务器上的单个 openlit.init() 调用工具；上下文交互和工具执行生成 OpenTelemetry 跨度。
  5. Grafana Cloud：收集的跟踪和指标流入 Grafana Cloud 完全托管的 Prometheus 和 Tempo 后端，其中专门的 MCP 仪表板提供协议使用情况的可见性。

第 1 步：安装 AI Observability 解决方案

首先将 AI Observability 集成添加到 Grafana Cloud堆栈。这可以通过点击左侧菜单中的连接并按照剩余的我们文档中概述的步骤来完成。

此配置预构建的仪表板，包括用于MCP 可观察性的一个，并配置了一个托管开放遥测协议 (OTLP) 网关来接收跟踪和指标。遥测数据流入后，仪表板会自动填充调用率、延迟百分位数和错误计数。

第 2 步：安装 OpenLIT 和 MCP 库

OpenLIT 为 MCP 以及 LLM、向量存储和代理框架提供自动检测。通过 pip 安装 OpenLIT 和 mcp 库（实现客户端和服务器）：

pip install openlit mcp

安装后，对 openlit.init() 的一次调用会自动检测所有 MCP 操作。如果您选择运行自己的遥测收集器而不是 Grafana 的 OTLP 网关，则 OpenLIT 可以通过 Docker Compose 自托管，或使用 OpenLIT Operator 部署到 Kubernetes。

步骤 3：检测您的 MCP 应用程序

检测只需两行代码。下面是公开 search_documents 工具的 MCP 服务器的简单示例。 OpenLIT 检测服务器，捕获每个工具调用和上下文交互：

import openlit
从 mcp 导入服务器
openlit.init() # 启用 OpenTelemetry 跟踪和指标
# 创建MCP服务器实例
服务器=服务器()
# 定义一个获取文档的工具
@server.tool(“搜索文档”)
def search_documents（查询：str）：
# 想象这个函数调用搜索 API 或数据库
结果=文档搜索（查询）
返回结果
# 在本地主机上运行 MCP 服务器
server.run（主机=“localhost”，端口=8080）
# 当客户端调用 search_documents 时，OpenLIT 捕获：
# * 上下文协议交互（例如，上下文加载和管理）
# * 工具使用指标（延迟和成功率）
# * 协议握手性能
# * 资源使用情况（上下文窗口大小、内存）
# * 错误或异常:contentReference[oaicite:14]{index=14}

要检测 MCP 客户端，请使用 mcp 库中的 Client 类，并在发出请求之前调用 openlit.init()：

import openlit
从 mcp 导入客户端
openlit.init()
客户端 = 客户端("http://localhost:8080")
tools = client.list_tools() # 列出可用工具
结果=客户。调用工具（
“搜索文档”，
{“query”：“人工智能可观察性”}
) # 调用该工具
# 所有客户端操作都会自动检测：contentReference[oaicite:15]{index=15}

OpenLIT 通过 CLI 包装器支持零代码检测。要在不修改代码的情况下检测现有 MCP 服务，请使用：

openlit-instrument \
--服务名称 my-mcp-app \
python your_mcp_app.py
# 使用自定义设置：
openlit-instrument \
--otlp-端点 http://127.0.0.1:4318 \
--服务名称 my-mcp-app \
--环境制作\
python your_mcp_app.py

步骤 4：配置环境变量

要将跟踪和指标发送到 Grafana Cloud，您需要 OpenTelemetry 凭据。从 Grafana Cloud 门户生成它们并在您的环境中设置以下变量：

export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://otlp-gateway-.grafana.net/otlp"
导出 OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="授权=基本 "
导出 OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="mcp-server-demo"
导出 OTEL_DEPLOYMENT_ENVIRONMENT="生产"

当您使用这些变量运行客户端或服务器时，OpenLIT SDK 会自动将遥测数据发送到 Grafana Cloud。

第 5 步：探索 MCP 可观测性仪表板

启动已检测的 MCP 客户端和服务器后，打开 Grafana Cloud 并导航到 AI 可观测性→MCP 可观测性。仪表板提供：

  • 工具性能：调用延迟直方图、成功率和每个工具的调用计数。
  • 协议运行状况：用于检测握手问题的会话稳定性和连接指标。
  • 资源使用情况：上下文窗口大小、内存和数据访问模式，帮助您优化服务器资源。
  • 错误跟踪：列出失败的操作以及跟踪 ID 和详细的异常信息以帮助调试

您可以通过查询 Prometheus 指标（例如工具调用持续时间）和 Tempo 跟踪来构建自定义仪表板。由于 OpenLIT 使用 OpenTelemetry，因此您不会被锁定到单个后端。您可以将遥测数据转发到任何与 OTLP 兼容的可观测性堆栈。

后续步骤

准备好了解更多信息了吗？在本系列的最后一篇博客中，我们将展示如何逐步进行设置，以实现 AI 可观测性的零代码检测方法。

您还可以在 官方文档，包括设置说明和仪表板。这些资源将帮助您从基本演示过渡到 AI 应用程序的生产就绪设置。

item.title: 【How to monitor LLMs in production with Grafana Cloud,OpenLIT, and OpenTelemetry】如何使用 Grafana Cloud、OpenLIT 和 OpenTelemetry 监控生产中的法学硕士
item.description:
【

Note: The world is changing all around us thanks to AI. Today, anyone and everyone can be a developer, using LLMs to create LLM-powered applications, which users can then interact with by using even more LLMs. 

Observability practitioners need to adapt and they need the right tools for the job. In this series, we'll show you how to use Grafana Cloud to monitor AI applications, including workloads in production (this post), AI agents, MCP servers, and zero-code LLMs.

Moving a large language model (LLM) application from a demo to a production‑scale service raises very different questions than the ones you ask when playing with an API key in a notebook.

In production, you have to answer: How much is each model costing us? Are we keeping latency within our service‑level objectives? Are we accidentally returning hallucinations or toxic content? Is the system vulnerable to prompt‑injection attacks? 

The good news is that modern observability tools such as Grafana Cloud enable you to answer these questions in a single place. In this guide, you'll learn how to set up end‑to‑end observability for generative‑AI workloads using the OpenLIT SDK and AI Observability in Grafana Cloud.

Why use Grafana Cloud for LLM observability?

AI Observability builds on Grafana Cloud's ability to visualize and query metrics, logs, traces, and profiles, and tailors it to the unique needs of AI workloads. Key capabilities include:

  • Unified GenAI monitoring: AI Observability tracks model latency, throughput, and availability, and surfaces user prompts and completions so developers can understand usage patterns. It also provides real‑time cost management and token analytics so teams can see how many input/output tokens are consumed and how much each call costs.
  • Quality and safety evaluations: The integration adds programmatic evaluators on top of your traces so you can create alerts for hallucinations, verify factual accuracy, and score content quality. It also monitors for toxicity, bias, and other safety issues. These evaluation signals can be used to gate deployments and alert operators when model quality drifts.
  • Full‑stack observability: In addition to LLM metrics, Grafana Cloud (using OpenLIT) monitors vector database operations, Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, and GPU performance. It tracks query latencies, resource utilization, and protocol health across the AI stack. There are also prebuilt dashboards covering five key areas: GenAI observability, GenAI evaluations, vector database observability, MCP observability, and GPU monitoring.
  • Vendor‑neutral instrumentation: The integration relies on OpenTelemetry, so you can export traces and metrics to any backend. Grafana Cloud provides a managed OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) gateway and fully managed Prometheus and Tempo services, eliminating the need to run your own observability stack.

These capabilities make Grafana Cloud a natural destination for AI telemetry. Combined with OpenLIT’s auto‑instrumentation, you can gain comprehensive insights with minimal code changes.

We use OpenLIT because it makes it easier to instrument AI applications with minimal setup, supporting 50+ GenAI tools such as LLMs, vector databases, and frameworks such as LangChain and CrewAI.

It is OpenTelemetry-native and follows the GenAI semantic conventions, which makes it a natural fit for Grafana Cloud and other OTel-based backends. The SDK generates traces and metrics for tokens, latency, and cost (including custom models), and the same integration can also be used to run evaluations.

Demo: How to configure Grafana Cloud to monitor a customer support bot

Let's build a practical example: a customer support chatbot that uses different LLM providers based on the query complexity. We'll monitor everything in Grafana Cloud, and by the end, you'll see how this integration can save you money on AI queries and dramatically reduce latency.

Note: if you get stuck anywhere along the way or need help with your own setup, click on the pulsar icon in the top-right corner of the Grafana Cloud UI to open a chat with Grafana Assistant, our purpose-built LLM that can help troubleshoot incidents, manage dashboards, and answer product questions.

Architecture

The high‑level architecture of an instrumented GenAI service is shown below. A router classifies incoming user messages and directs them to the most appropriate model. Every call, regardless of provider, is instrumented by OpenLIT, which generates traces and metrics. These signals are forwarded via the OTLP gateway to Grafana Cloud, where pre‑built dashboards visualize performance, cost, and quality.

User Query → Route by complexity → [GPT-4 (complex) | Claude (medium) | GPT-3.5 (simple)]
↓
OpenLIT SDK captures traces & metrics
↓
Grafana Cloud (Grafana Cloud Metrics + Grafana Cloud Traces + Grafana Cloud Logs)
↓
AI Observability Dashboards

Step 1: Install AI Observability

Start by adding AI Observability to your Grafana Cloud stack. This can be done by clicking on Connections in the left-side menu and following the steps outlined in our documentation.

This installs the five dashboards mentioned earlier (GenAI observability, GenAI evaluations, vector DB observability, MCP observability, and GPU monitoring). When metrics arrive, these dashboards automatically populate with latency histograms, token counts, cost summaries, and evaluation results.

Step 2: Install OpenLIT

Install OpenLIT and your preferred model providers via pip:

pip install openlit openai anthropic cohere

This command pulls the latest OpenLIT SDK from PyPI (v1.35.9 at the time of writing) and any client libraries you need for your models.

Step 3: Instrument your application

The simplest way to add observability is to call openlit.init() at the beginning of your application. You can optionally pass an service_name and environment to improve dashboard organization.

Below is a more realistic example than the original single‑model snippet. The router uses simple logic to choose between GPT‑3.5, Claude 3, and GPT‑4 based on message complexity. OpenLIT instruments every API call automatically. We also demonstrate how to use the evaluation and guardrail APIs to flag hallucinations and prompt‑injection attempts:

import os
import openlit
from openai import OpenAI
from anthropic import Anthropic
# Initialize OpenLIT with an application name and environment
openlit.init()
# Initialize clients for each model provider
openai_client = OpenAI(api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
anthropic_client = Anthropic(api_key=os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"))
def route_query(message: str) -> str:
"""Select a model based on the message length/complexity."""
words = len(message.split())
if words < 20:
return "gpt-3.5-turbo" # simple queries
elif words < 100:
return "claude-3-haiku" # medium complexity
else:
return "gpt-4-turbo" # complex queries
def call_model(model_name: str, message: str) -> str:
"""Send the query to the selected LLM.
OpenLIT will automatically instrument each API call.
"""
if model_name.startswith("gpt-3.5") or model_name.startswith("gpt-4"):
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
model=model_name,
messages=[{"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
{"role": "user", "content": message}]
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
elif model_name.startswith("claude"):
response = anthropic_client.completions.create(
model=model_name,
prompt=f"\n\nHuman: {message}\n\nAssistant:",
max_tokens=1024
)
return response.completion
else:
raise ValueError(f"Unsupported model: {model_name}")
def chat(user_message: str) -> str:
# Choose the right model for the query
model = route_query(user_message)
answer = call_model(model, user_message)
# Optional: Evaluate the response for hallucinations and injection attempts
evals = openlit.evals.Hallucination(provider="openai", api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
# Evaluation metric is automatically sent to the configured OTel destination
evals.measure(prompt=user_message,
contexts=["Internal knowledge base"],
text=answer)
# Guard against prompt injection or sensitive topics
guard = openlit.guard.All(provider="openai", api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
guard.detect(text=user_message)
return answer
if __name__ == "__main__":
user_question = input("Ask our support bot a question: ")
print(chat(user_question))

This example shows how to route requests to different models and still emit consistent traces and metrics. We also run a hallucination evaluator and a combined guardrail on each message. The hallucination evaluator detects factual inaccuracies, contradictions, and fabricated information, while the All guardrail simultaneously performs injection detection, sensitive‑topic filtering, and topic restriction.

Step 4: Run the application

To send data to Grafana Cloud, you need an OTLP endpoint and an API token. To do so, log in to your Grafana Cloud stack, open the OpenTelemetry settings, generate an API token, and copy the OTEL endpoint and headers. 

Export these values as environment variables before starting your application:

export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=my-ai-app
export OTEL_DEPLOYMENT_ENVIRONMENT=production
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://otlp-gateway-.grafana.net/otlp"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="Authorization=Basic "
export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-openai-key"
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-anthropic-key"
python your_app.py

Replace with your Grafana zone (for example, prod-us-central-0) and with the base64‑encoded instance ID and API token. When you run your instrumented application, OpenLIT will connect to the OTLP gateway and begin sending traces and metrics.

Step 5: Visualize in Grafana Cloud

Once data flows into Grafana, open the AI Observability dashboards. The GenAI observability dashboard visualizes request rates, latency percentiles, and cost metrics. For instance, it tracks "time to first token" and overall latency across providers and surfaces the total and average cost per request using metrics such as gen_ai_usage_cost_USD_sum and gen_ai_usage_input_tokens_total. The GenAI evaluations dashboard summarizes hallucination, bias, and toxicity detection events.

Grafana Alerting can trigger notifications when costs exceed thresholds, latency spikes, or evaluation scores cross your quality gates. Because everything is built on OpenTelemetry metrics, you can also build custom panels and alerts tailored to your use case.

The value you get

Now that we've walked through how to use AI Observability in Grafana Cloud, let's look at some hypothetical scenarios to illustrate the actionable insights to help improve application performance.

Cost optimization

Before: "Our AI costs are going up, but we don't know why."

After: You can see:

  • GPT-5 accounts for 70% of costs but only 20% of queries
  • Switching simple queries to GPT-4 saves $2000/month
  • One user is making excessive API calls

Performance monitoring

Before: "Users complain the bot is slow sometimes."

After: You discover:

  • Claude has 30% lower TTFT than GPT-4
  • Latency spikes correlate with Claude API rate limits
  • 95th percentile latency is 3.2s (above your 3s SLA)

Quality assurance

Before: "Why are our users not happy with AI responses?"

After: You can see:

  • Hallucinations appear in 20% of the requests
  • Answer accuracy dropped from 92% to 81% after the latest prompt change
  • Instruction-following failures increased to 15% for complex queries

Debugging complex issues

With distributed tracing, you can:

- Follow a request from user input → classification → LLM call → response

- See exact prompts that caused errors

- Identify which part of your pipeline is slow

- Correlate issues with specific users or time periods

Next steps

Want to go further? In the next blog in this series, we’ll show how to set this up, step by step, for an agentic AI application.

You can also learn more about AI Observability in the official docs, including setup instructions and dashboards.These resources will help you move from a basic demo to a production-ready setup for your AI applications in no time.

】

注意：由于人工智能，我们周围的世界正在发生变化。如今，任何人都可以成为开发人员，使用 LLM 来创建由 LLM 驱动的应用程序，然后用户可以使用更多的 LLM 与其进行交互。 

可观察性从业者需要适应，他们需要合适的工具来完成工作。在本系列中，我们将向您展示如何使用 Grafana Cloud 监控 AI 应用程序，包括生产中的工作负载（本文）、AI 代理、MCP 服务器和零代码LLM。

将大型语言模型 (LLM) 应用程序从演示迁移到生产规模服务会引发与在笔记本中使用 API 密钥时提出的问题截然不同的问题。

在生产中，您必须回答：每个模型要花多少钱？我们是否将延迟控制在我们的服务水平目标范围内？我们是否意外地返回了幻觉或有毒内容？系统是否容易受到即时注入攻击？ 

好消息是 Grafana Cloud 等现代可观察性工具使您能够在一个地方回答这些问题。在本指南中，您将了解如何使用 OpenLIT SDK 和 AI 为生成式 AI 工作负载设置端到端可观察性Grafana Cloud 中的可观测性。

为什么使用 Grafana Cloud 实现 LLM 可观测性？

AI Observability 建立在 Grafana Cloud 可视化和查询指标、日志、跟踪和配置文件的能力之上，并根据 AI 工作负载的独特需求进行定制。主要功能包括：

  • 统一 GenAI 监控：AI Observability 跟踪模型延迟、吞吐量和可用性，并显示用户提示和完成情况，以便开发人员了解使用模式。它还提供实时成本管理和令牌分析，以便团队可以查看消耗了多少输入/输出令牌以及每次调用的成本。
  • 质量和安全评估：集成在您的跟踪之上添加了程序化评估器，以便您可以创建幻觉警报、验证事实准确性并对内容质量进行评分。它还监控毒性、偏见和其他安全问题。这些评估信号可用于控制部署并在模型质量出现偏差时向操作员发出警报。
  • 全栈可观察性：除了LLM指标之外，Grafana Cloud（使用OpenLIT）还监控矢量数据库操作、模型上下文协议（MCP）服务器和GPU性能。它跟踪整个人工智能堆栈的查询延迟、资源利用率和协议运行状况。还有涵盖五个关键领域的预构建仪表板：GenAI 可观测性、GenAI 评估、矢量数据库可观测性、MCP 可观测性和 GPU 监控。
  • 供应商中立仪器：集成依赖于 OpenTelemetry，因此您可以将跟踪和指标导出到任何后端。 Grafana Cloud 提供托管 OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) 网关和完全托管的 Prometheus 和 Tempo 服务，无需运行您自己的可观测性堆栈。

这些功能使 Grafana Cloud 成为 AI 遥测的天然目的地。与 OpenLIT 的自动检测相结合，您可以通过最少的代码更改获得全面的见解。

我们使用 OpenLIT 是因为它可以更轻松地以最少的设置来检测 AI 应用程序，支持 50 多种 GenAI 工具（例如 LLM、向量数据库以及 LangChain 和 CrewAI 等框架）。

它是 OpenTelemetry 原生的，遵循 GenAI 语义约定，这使其非常适合 Grafana Cloud 和其他基于 OTel 的后端。 SDK 生成令牌、延迟和成本（包括自定义模型）的跟踪和指标，并且相同的集成也可用于运行评估。

演示：如何配置 Grafana Cloud 来监控客户支持机器人

让我们构建一个实际示例：根据查询复杂性使用不同 LLM 提供商的客户支持聊天机器人。我们将监控 Grafana Cloud 中的所有内容，到最后，您将看到此集成如何为您节省 AI 查询费用并显着减少延迟。

注意：如果您在任何地方遇到困难或需要自己设置方面的帮助，请单击 Grafana Cloud UI 右上角的 pulsar 图标以打开与 Grafana Assistant，我们专门构建的法学硕士，可以帮助解决事件、管理仪表板和回答产品问题。

架构

仪表化 GenAI 服务的高级架构如下所示。路由器对传入的用户消息进行分类，并将其引导至最合适的模型。每个调用，无论提供商如何，都会由 OpenLIT 进行检测，生成跟踪和指标。这些信号通过 OTLP 网关转发到 Grafana Cloud，其中预构建的仪表板可视化性能、成本和质量。

<代码>用户查询→按复杂性路由→[GPT-4（复杂）|克劳德（中）| GPT-3.5（简单）]
↓
OpenLIT SDK 捕获跟踪和指标
↓
Grafana Cloud（Grafana Cloud 指标 + Grafana Cloud 跟踪 + Grafana Cloud 日志）
↓
AI 可观察性仪表板

第 1 步：安装 AI Observability

首先将 AI Observability 添加到 Grafana Cloud 堆栈中。这可以通过点击左侧菜单中的连接并按照我们文档中列出的步骤来完成。

这将安装前面提到的五个仪表板（GenAI 可观测性、GenAI 评估、矢量 DB 可观测性、MCP 可观测性和 GPU 监控）。当指标到达时，这些仪表板会自动填充延迟直方图、令牌计数、成本摘要和评估结果。

第 2 步：安装 OpenLIT

通过 pip 安装 OpenLIT 和您的首选模型提供程序：

pip install openlit openai anthropic cohere

此命令从 PyPI 中提取最新的 OpenLIT SDK（撰写本文时为 v1.35.9）以及模型所需的任何客户端库。

第 3 步：检测您的应用程序

添加可观察性的最简单方法是在应用程序开头调用 openlit.init()。您可以选择传递 service_name 和 environment 来改进仪表板组织。

下面是一个比原始单模型片段更实际的示例。路由器使用简单的逻辑根据消息复杂性在 GPT-3.5、Claude 3 和 GPT-4 之间进行选择。 OpenLIT 自动检测每个 API 调用。我们还演示了如何使用评估和防护 API 来标记幻觉和提示注入尝试：

import os
导入开光
从 openai 导入 OpenAI
从 anthropic 导入 Anthropic
# 使用应用程序名称和环境初始化 OpenLIT
openlit.init()
# 为每个模型提供者初始化客户端
openai_client = OpenAI(api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
anthropic_client = Anthropic(api_key=os.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"))
def route_query(消息: str) -> str:
"""根据消息长度/复杂性选择模型。"""
单词 = len(message.split())
如果单词数 < 20：
return "gpt-3.5-turbo" # 简单查询
elif 字数 < 100：
return "claude-3-haiku" # 中等复杂度
其他：
return "gpt-4-turbo" # 复杂查询
def call_model(model_name: str, message: str) -> str:
"""将查询发送至所选的法学硕士。
OpenLIT 将自动检测每个 API 调用。
”“”
如果 model_name.startswith("gpt-3.5") 或 model_name.startswith("gpt-4")：
响应 = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
模型=模型名称，
messages=[{"role": "system", "content": "你是一个有用的助手。"},
{“角色”：“用户”，“内容”：消息}]
）
返回response.choices[0].message.content
elif model_name.startswith(“克劳德”):
响应 = anthropic_client.completions.create(
模型=模型名称，
Prompt=f"\n\n人：{消息}\n\n助理：",
最大令牌=1024
）
返回response.completion
其他：
raise ValueError(f"不支持的模型：{model_name}")
def chat(user_message: str) -> str:
# 为查询选择正确的模型
模型 = 路线查询（用户消息）
答案 = call_model(模型, user_message)
# 可选：评估幻觉和注射尝试的反应
evals = openlit.evals.Hallucination(provider="openai", api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
# 评估指标自动发送到配置的 OTel 目的地
evals.measure(提示=user_message,
contexts=["内部知识库"],
文字=答案）
# 防范提示注入或敏感话题
Guard = openlit.guard.All(provider="openai", api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
Guard.Detect(文本=用户消息)
返回答案
如果 __name__ == "__main__":
user_question = input(“向我们的支持机器人询问一个问题：”)
打印（聊天（用户问题））

此示例展示了如何将请求路由到不同的模型并仍然发出一致的跟踪和指标。我们还在每条消息上运行幻觉评估器和组合护栏。幻觉评估器检测事实不准确、矛盾和伪造信息，而 All 护栏同时执行注入检测、敏感主题过滤和主题限制。

第 4 步：运行应用程序

要将数据发送到 Grafana Cloud，您需要 OTLP 端点和 API 令牌。为此，请登录 Grafana Cloud 堆栈，打开 OpenTelemetry 设置，生成 API 令牌，然后复制 OTEL 端点和标头。 

在启动应用程序之前将这些值导出为环境变量：

export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=my-ai-app
导出 OTEL_DEPLOYMENT_ENVIRONMENT=生产
导出 OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://otlp-gateway-<区域>.grafana.net/otlp"
导出 OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="授权=基本 "
导出 OPENAI_API_KEY="your-openai-key"
导出 ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="your-anthropic-key"
python your_app.py

将 替换为您的 Grafana 区域（例如 prod-us-central-0），并将 替换为 Base64 编码的实例 ID 和 API 令牌。当您运行已检测的应用程序时，OpenLIT 将连接到 OTLP 网关并开始发送跟踪和指标。

第 5 步：在 Grafana Cloud 中可视化

数据流入 Grafana 后，打开 AI Observability 仪表板。 GenAI 可观测性仪表板以可视化方式显示请求率、延迟百分位数和成本指标。例如，它跟踪“第一个令牌的时间”和跨提供商的总体延迟，并使用 gen_ai_usage_cost_USD_sum 和 gen_ai_usage_input_tokens_total。 GenAI 评估仪表板总结了幻觉、偏差和毒性检测事件。

Grafana 警报可以在成本超过阈值、延迟峰值或评估分数超过质量门时触发通知。由于一切都是基于 OpenTelemetry 指标构建的，因此您还可以根据您的用例构建自定义面板和警报。

您获得的价值

现在我们已经了解了如何在 Grafana Cloud 中使用 AI Observability，让我们看一些假设场景，以说明有助于提高应用程序性能的可行见解。

成本优化

之前：“我们的 AI 成本是上升，但我们不知道为什么。”

之后：您可以看到：

  • GPT-5 占成本的 70%，但只占查询的 20%
  • 将简单查询切换到 GPT-4 每月节省 2000 美元
  • 一个用户进行过多的 API 调用

性能监控

之前：“用户抱怨机器人有时很慢。”

之后：您发现：

  • Claude 的 TTFT 比 GPT-4 低 30%
  • 延迟峰值与 Claude API 速率限制相关
  • 95% 的延迟为 3.2 秒（高于您的 3 秒） SLA）

质量保证

之前：“为什么我们的用户对 AI 响应不满意？”

之后：您可以看到：

  • 20% 的请求出现幻觉
  • 最新提示后，答案准确率从 92% 下降到 81%更改
  • 复杂查询的指令跟踪失败率增加到 15%

调试复杂问题

通过分布式跟踪，您可以：

- 遵循用户输入 → 分类 → LLM 调用 → 响应的请求

- 查看导致错误的确切提示

- 确定管道的哪一部分速度较慢

- 将问题与特定用户或特定用户相关联时间段

后续步骤

想要更进一步吗？在本系列的下一篇博客中，我们将逐步展示如何针对代理 AI 应用程序进行设置。

您还可以在官方文档，包括设置说明和仪表板。这些资源将帮助您立即从基本演示转变为 AI 应用程序的生产就绪设置。

item.title: 【Open standards in 2026: The backbone of modern observability】2026 年的开放标准：现代可观测性的支柱
item.description:
【

Open source software and open standards are now an essential part of how organizations maintain their systems.

That's not to say they haven't always been important, but the fourth annual Observability Survey, brought to you by Grafana Labs, shows just how deeply the shift to open has taken hold, with 77% of respondents saying open source and open standards are important1 to their observability strategy. We're also seeing open source have an impact on how people prioritize and select new tools, while OpenTelemetry continues to become a unified standard across all telemetry signals.

Taken collectively, this points to a broader change: Organizations are shifting from strategies anchored to a single vendor to strategies that prioritize open, portable standards for moving data across tools and platforms to better understand the state of their systems—and even their businesses.

In this blog post, we'll take a closer look at the role of open source and open standards in observability today, based on the results from the 2026 edition of the Observability Survey, the largest community-driven survey on the state of the industry.

Note: Check out the full 2026 Observability Survey to see what more than 1,300 respondents have to say about AI, open source, the expanding scope of observability, and the practices organizations are adopting today. And watch the video below for a recap on some of the main themes coming out of this year's report. 

You can also read our in-depth analysis on the role of AI in observability, or dig into the stats yourself in our Grafana dashboard, which includes filters for industry, role, region, and company size so your team compares to its peers.

Open source and open standards are central to observability strategies today

Companies today are collecting so much telemetry from so many disparate sources, and they need a way to unify that data in one central location. As a result, open source and open standards have become mainstream. In fact, 61% of respondents say open source/open standards are either “very important” or “essential” to their observability strategy. Only 3% say they’re not important at all.

Open is more important to companies that prioritize observability as part of a broader business strategy, too. Among respondents who say observability is business-critical at the highest levels of their company ("CTO/C-level"), 64% cite open source/open standards as "very important" or "essential." In contrast, among those who say observability is "not at all" considered critical at their company, just 51% cite open source/open standards are "very important" or "essential."

Teams that place greater value on open source/open standards are also more likely to save time or money through centralized observability, with 84% of those who say it's "essential" reporting time/money savings, compared to 77% of all respondents.

And while teams that use self-managed setups are much more likely to see open as important, a majority of those using SaaS say it's important, too, including 61% of those using "SaaS only" and 73% of those using "mostly SaaS." Most SaaS platforms that dominated the early days of observability all used proprietary collectors and backends, so this broad support for open is yet another sign of the shifting dynamic in this space today.

Open source, open standards, and the focus on outcomes

When asked about selecting new observability tools, a quarter of respondents say being "based on open source software/technologies" is one of their most important criteria, making it the fourth most common consideration behind cost, ease of use, and interoperability with other tools. 

That puts "open source" in the top third of selection criteria (respondents could pick up to three), but it's also well short of the percentage of people who say "open source/open standards" are important1 to their observability strategy (77%). There are many possible reasons for this, but part of it likely comes down to how people separate open source from open standards, with the former typically associated with backends and visualization layers and the latter associated with data collection frameworks. 

The two de facto open standards in observability today are Prometheus and OpenTelemetry. Yes, they're both open source projects, but they're perceived differently than the components that make up an open source observability stack. 

Note: It's worth mentioning here that OpenTelemetry, as a project, aspires to be a much broader standard to work across all telemetry types, while Prometheus is focused squarely on metrics. You don't have to standardize across your entire architecture to get the full value out of Prometheus the same way you would with OpenTelemetry. Check out this recent "Big Tent" podcast to hear more on this topic.

And while we didn't explicitly ask about open standards as part of the tool-selection criteria, it's clear practitioners value the benefits that come from using them. For example, "interoperability with other tools at my organization" (26%), "cross-observability signal correlation" (15%), and "ease of switching to another tool in the future" (12%), can all be tied back to the benefits of open standards. And when you combine those answers with "based on open source software/technologies," 58% of all respondents—and 62% of those who say open is important1 to their observability strategy—cite at least one of them as a top criteria.

Open source and AI

You may have noticed the chart in the previous section that stated only 15% of respondents cite "AI capabilities" as a top priority when selecting new observability tools. This survey was conducted between October and January, so it's likely that opinions have shifted since then, given the rapid pace of development in this space.

It's also worth calling out the emerging relationship between open source, observability, and AI. Grafana Labs CTO Tom Wilkie recently called open source the "cheat code" for AI. He noted that LLMs are trained on vast amounts of publicly available information about open source projects, so they're essentially "pre-wired with knowledge of how to interact with these open ecosystems"—something that helped accelerate the development of our own LLM, Grafana Assistant. 

We're also seeing a lot of interest in our open source MCP server, which gives AI agents and LLMs comprehensive access to Grafana and its observability ecosystem. Clearly AI and open source will continue to evolve and influence one another, so this will definitely be a space to keep an eye on.

Prometheus is deeply embedded, and OpenTelemetry is quickly catching up 

If open standards are the foundation of modern observability, then Prometheus and OpenTelemetry are the main pillars of that foundation. Both projects are seeing widespread investments across organizations of all sizes and industries. In fact, 65% of respondents report investing (investigating, building POCs, or using in production) in both Prometheus and OpenTelemetry. 

Prometheus holds a slight lead in overall adoption (77% vs. 76%), but there are notable differences in how people are deploying the two technologies. Prometheus is used much more in production2 (59% vs. 41%), which makes sense since it's a more mature technology. OpenTelemetry has a greater share of people investigating or building POCs (35% vs. 18%), meaning it has a larger pipeline of organizations preparing to scale their OpenTelemetry deployments.

There's also a split in the degree to which teams use OpenTelemetry and Prometheus in production. Both are used for "some production workloads" at the same rate (20%), but Prometheus is used for the "majority of" or "all" workloads at almost twice the rate. This could be attributed to Prometheus being more established, but it also points to a potential missed opportunity. Certain parts of OpenTelemetry—more specifically, traces—are less useful when you don't have everything fully instrumented, so it's possible people aren't yet seeing the full benefits of moving to OpenTelemetry. 

Regardless, each of the projects is seeing positive momentum, as organizations were more likely to have increased their usage of both over the past year. The percentage of respondents who say they increased their usage of OpenTelemetry was slightly higher (47% vs. 42%), highlighting how it's at an earlier stage of adoption, with a higher trajectory for potential growth. Only a small minority decreased their use of Prometheus (6%) and OpenTelemetry (4%) compared to the previous year.

OpenTelemetry is delivering on its potential for metrics, logs, and traces

While Prometheus was designed exclusively for metrics, OpenTelemetry has been built to do more as a single, vendor-neutral standard for metrics, logs, traces, and even profiles (though profiles are still an emerging space for the project and observability in general). And that potential is quickly becoming reality, as respondents say they use OpenTelemetry broadly across all three major telemetry types—metrics (57%), traces (50%), and logs (48%).

Interestingly, organizations using SaaS are more likely to deploy OpenTelemetry today. For example, when you compare "SaaS only" to "self-managed only" users, they're more likely to use it for metrics (65% vs. 51%), traces (52% vs. 46%), and logs (52% vs. 45%). However, self-managed users are generally more likely to have increased their investments across all three telemetry types over the past year. This points to two possible explanations—either SaaS enables teams to move quicker, or teams that want to move quicker are inclined to opt for SaaS.

Teams want OpenTelemetry to deliver simpler observability—and more freedom

So why are organizations embracing OpenTelemetry? Yes, the promise of support for metrics, logs and traces is important—as is the need for semantic conventions—but it's clear that the technical specs are less important than the intended outcomes. In fact, it really came down to two things: simplicity and flexibility.

"Ease of adoption and management" (41%) and "ease of switching vendors and backend technologies" (37%) were the clear leaders in terms of what respondents want to solve with OpenTelemetry—and for good reason. Moving to a new observability platform often means re-instrumenting applications, rebuilding telemetry pipelines, and retraining employees, but with OpenTelemetry you can instrument once and send data anywhere.

That type of functionality will become more important  as the scope of observability expands. For example, half of all respondents report using observability tools to track business-related metrics. That type of data is traditionally analyzed in BI platforms, so standardized pipelines will be essential if teams want to correlate that data with other telemetry signals.

And the more teams pin their future to OpenTelemetry, the more likely they are to see it as a potential solution to these two challenges. Taken together, the results point to a growing desire for observability systems that are easier to use, more flexible, and less tied to any single vendor.

Final thoughts on the Observability Survey and open source

We want to thank everyone who participated in this year's survey, which was our biggest yet. Between October and January, more than 1,300 observability practitioners and leaders from 76 different countries participated in our survey. We collected responses online and at events around the world, including our own shows and third-party conferences such as AWS re:Invent and KubeCON.

It speaks to the collective power of the open source community that so many of you were willing to share your thoughts, and we take pride in making the Observability Survey an ungated, public asset for anyone who wants to learn from and share the data.

We've built some of the most popular open source technologies available today, including Grafana and Loki, and we've been major contributors to both Prometheus and OpenTelemetry. Listening to your feedback is a core to our understanding of Grafana Labs as an open source company.  We learn from you how to do better work. Thank you for sharing your knowledge and opinions with us.

1 "Somewhat important," "very important," and "essential" answers combined

2  "We are using it across a few production workloads," "we are using it across the majority of production workloads," and "we are using it in all production workloads" answers combined

】

开源软件和开放标准现在是组织维护系统的重要组成部分。

这并不是说它们并不总是很重要，但 Grafana Labs 为您带来的第四次年度可观测性调查显示了向开放的转变已经深入人心，77% 的受访者表示开源和开放标准对于他们的可观测性策略很重要1。我们还看到开源对人们如何优先考虑和选择新工具产生影响，而 OpenTelemetry 继续成为所有遥测信号的统一标准。

总的来说，这意味着更广泛的变化：组织正在从以单一供应商为基础的战略转向优先考虑开放、可移植标准的战略，以便跨工具和平台移动数据，以更好地了解其系统甚至业务的状态。

在这篇博文中，我们将仔细研究基于 2026 年版可观测性调查的结果，这是当今可观测性领域的开源和开放标准，这是关于行业状况的最大的社区驱动调查。

注意：查看完整的2026 年可观测性调查，了解更多内容1,300 名受访者不得不谈论人工智能、开源、可观测范围的扩大以及组织如今采用的实践。观看下面的视频，回顾今年报告中的一些主题。 

您还可以阅读我们对人工智能在可观测性中的作用的深入分析，或者在我们的Grafana仪表板中自行深入研究统计数据，其中包括行业、角色、地区和公司规模的过滤器，以便您的团队与同行进行比较。

开源和开放标准是当今可观察性策略的核心

当今的公司从如此多不同的来源收集如此多的遥测数据，他们需要一种方法将这些数据统一到一个中心位置。结果，开源和开放标准已成为主流。事实上，61% 的受访者表示开源/开放标准对于他们的可观察性策略“非常重要”或“必不可少”。只有 3% 的人表示它们根本不重要。

对于将可观察性作为更广泛业务战略一部分的公司来说，开放性更为重要。在表示可观察性对于公司最高层（“CTO/C 级”）至关重要的受访者中，64% 的人认为开源/开放标准“非常重要”或“必要”。相比之下，在那些认为可观察性在他们的公司中“一点也不重要”的人中，只有 51% 的人认为开源/开放标准“非常重要”或“必要”。

团队认为对开源/开放标准给予更大重视的人也更有可能通过集中可观察性来节省时间或金钱，84% 的受访者表示这是“必要的”节省时间/金钱，而所有受访者中这一比例为 77%。

虽然使用自我管理设置的团队更有可能认为开放很重要，但大多数使用 SaaS 的团队也表示这很重要，包括 61% 使用“仅 SaaS”的团队和 73% 的团队那些“主要使用 SaaS”的人。早期主导可观察性的大多数 SaaS 平台都使用专有收集器和后端，因此对开放的广泛支持是当今该领域动态转变的另一个标志。

开源、开放标准和对结果的关注

当被问及选择新的可观察性工具时，四分之一的受访者表示“基于开源软件/技术”是他们最重要的标准之一，使其成为仅次于成本、易用性和可用性的第四大最常见考虑因素。与其他工具的互操作性。 

这使得“开源”成为选择标准的前三分之一（受访者最多可以选择三个），但这也远远低于认为“开源/开放标准”对其可观察性策略很重要的人的百分比（77%）。造成这种情况的可能原因有很多，但部分原因可能在于人们如何将开源与开放标准分开，前者通常与后端和可视化层相关，后者与数据收集框架相关。 

当今可观测性方面事实上的两个开放标准是 Prometheus 和 OpenTelemetry。是的，它们都是开源项目，但人们对它们的看法与构成开源可观察性堆栈的组件不同。 

注意：这里值得一提的是，OpenTelemetry 作为一个项目，渴望成为一个适用于所有遥测类型的更广泛的标准，而 Prometheus 则完全专注于指标。您无需像使用 OpenTelemetry 那样对整个架构进行标准化即可充分发挥 Prometheus 的价值。请观看这个最近的“大帐篷”播客了解有关此主题的更多信息。

虽然我们没有明确询问开放标准作为工具选择标准的一部分，但很明显，从业者重视使用它们所带来的好处。例如，“与我组织中其他工具的互操作性”（26%）、“交叉可观察性信号相关性”（15%）和“将来切换到另一个工具的便捷性”（12%）都可以与开放标准的好处联系起来。当您将这些答案与“基于开源软件/技术”结合起来时，58% 的受访者（以及 62% 表示开放对其可观察性策略很重要1的受访者）至少引用其中一项作为首要标准。

开放资源和人工智能

您可能已经注意到上一节中的图表，其中显示只有 15% 的受访者在选择新的可观察性工具时将“人工智能功能”作为首要考虑因素。这项调查是在 10 月到 1 月之间进行的，因此考虑到该领域的快速发展，自那以后观点可能发生了变化。

还值得指出开源、可观察性和人工智能之间正在出现的关系。 Grafana Labs 首席技术官 Tom Wilkie 最近称开源人工智能的“作弊代码”。他指出，法学硕士接受了大量有关开源项目的公开信息的培训，因此他们本质上“预先掌握了如何与这些开放生态系统交互的知识”——这有助于加速我们自己的法学硕士 Grafana Assistant 的开发。 

我们还看到人们对我们的开源 MCP 服务器很感兴趣，它使人工智能代理和法学硕士能够全面访问 Grafana 及其可观测性生态系统。显然，人工智能和开源将继续发展并相互影响，因此这绝对是一个值得关注的领域。

Prometheus 已深入人心，而 OpenTelemetry 正在迅速迎头赶上

如果开放标准是现代可观测性的基础，那么 Prometheus 和 OpenTelemetry 就是该基础的主要支柱。这两个项目都得到了各种规模和行业组织的广泛投资。事实上，65% 的受访者表示对 Prometheus 和 OpenTelemetry 进行了投资（调查、构建 POC 或在生产中使用）。 

Prometheus 在整体采用率方面略有领先（77% 与 76%），但人们部署这两种技术的方式存在显着差异。 Prometheus 在生产中的使用率要高得多2（59% vs. 41%），这是有道理的，因为它是一项更成熟的技术。 OpenTelemetry 调查或构建 POC 的人员比例更高（35% 比 18%），这意味着有更多的组织准备扩展其 OpenTelemetry 部署。

团队在生产中使用 OpenTelemetry 和 Prometheus 的程度也存在差异。两者都以相同的速率 (20%) 用于“某些生产工作负载”，但 Prometheus 以几乎两倍的速率用于“大多数”或“所有”工作负载。这可能是由于普罗米修斯更加成熟，但这也表明它可能错失了机会。当您没有完全检测所有内容时，OpenTelemetry 的某些部分（更具体地说，跟踪）就不太有用，因此人们可能还没有看到迁移到 OpenTelemetry 的全部好处。 

无论如何，每个项目都看到了积极的势头，因为组织在过去的一年里，他们更有可能增加对两者的使用。表示增加了 OpenTelemetry 使用率的受访者比例略高（47% 比 42%），这凸显了它处于采用的早期阶段，具有更高的潜在增长轨迹。与上一年相比，只有一小部分人减少了 Prometheus (6%) 和 OpenTelemetry (4%) 的使用。

OpenTelemetry 正在发挥其在指标、日志和跟踪方面的潜力

虽然 Prometheus 是专门为指标而设计的，但 OpenTelemetry 的目的是作为指标、日志、跟踪甚至配置文件的单一、供应商中立的标准（尽管配置文件仍然是该项目和配置文件的一个新兴领域）一般可观察性）。这种潜力正在迅速成为现实，因为受访者表示他们在所有三种主要遥测类型中广泛使用 OpenTelemetry：指标 (57%)、跟踪 (50%) 和日志 (48%)。

有趣的是，如今使用 SaaS 的组织更有可能部署 OpenTelemetry。例如，当您将“仅限 SaaS”与“仅限自我管理”用户进行比较时，他们更有可能将其用于指标（65% vs. 51%）、跟踪（52% vs. 46%）和日志（52% vs. 45%）。然而，自我管理用户通常更有可能在过去一年中增加对所有三种遥测类型的投资。这指向两种可能的解释：要么 SaaS 使团队能够更快地行动，要么想要更快地行动的团队倾向于选择 SaaS。

团队希望 OpenTelemetry 提供更简单的可观察性和更多的自由

那么为什么组织要拥抱 OpenTelemetry？是的，对指标、日志和跟踪的支持承诺很重要，就像对语义约定的需求一样，但很明显，技术规范不如预期结果重要。事实上，这实际上可以归结为两件事：简单性和灵活性。

就受访者希望通过 OpenTelemetry 解决的问题而言，“易于采用和管理”(41%) 和“易于更换供应商和后端技术”(37%) 是明显的领先者，并且有充分的理由。迁移到新的可观测平台通常意味着重新检测应用程序、重建遥测管道和重新培训员工，但使用 OpenTelemetry，您可以检测一次并将数据发送到任何地方。

随着可观测范围的扩大，这种类型的功能将变得更加重要。例如，一半的受访者表示使用可观察性工具来跟踪业务相关指标。传统上，此类数据是在 BI 平台中进行分析的，因此，如果团队希望将这些数据与其他遥测信号相关联，标准化管道将至关重要。

越多的团队将未来寄托在 OpenTelemetry 上，他们就越有可能将其视为解决这两个挑战的潜在解决方案。综合起来，结果表明 g人们渴望更易于使用、更灵活、更少依赖于任何单一供应商的可观测性系统。
